---
title: "Practice-based Education Data Science: A Proposal for Our Emerging Field"
subtitle: "A keynote presentation at the Stanford Education Data Science conference"
author: "Joshua M. Rosenberg, Ph.D."
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [simple, custom.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    incremental: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    transition: none
    auto-stretch: false
    code-block-bg: false
---

# What is Education Data Science, Really?

## What do you _mean_ by ...

<div style="text-align:center">
***My goal is to improve educational _outcomes_.***
***My goal is to _improve_ educational outcomes.***
***My _goal_ is to improve educational outcomes.***
</div>

::: {.notes}
I have a friend, and maybe you have a friend like this (or this person is you!) who is always interrogating and critiquing the subtle framing of research. If I say, for example, my goal is to improve educational outcomes, they will ask, of course:

which outcomes? A totally reasonable question. 

But then they will also ask, what do you mean by improve? A harder question.

And, even, what do you mean by "my goal"? At this point, I often found myself provoked and ready to abandon the conversation, but, on reflection, the questions are always thoughtful. 

This talk is meant (or should I say, I mean for this talk!) to get a bit at something related - what are the underlying goals of what we call educational data science?
:::

## Perspectives

![](/img/rmldt-2.png){width="70%"}

::: {.notes}
But maybe that wasn't quite it --- it wasn't so much about method and pedagogy as about _in_ vs. _for_ education

This talk argues the choice is impo and timely.
:::

## The question motivating this talk

<div style="text-align:center">
## A question

***Is EDS a methodological program that takes education as a use case, or is it work that is situated in educational contexts and accountable to its educational impacts?***
</div>

# My pathway to the question

## My background

![](/img/jr-classroom.jpg){width="70%"}

::: {.notes}
I'll share very briefly where I am coming from. I was a science teacher, and I intended for that to be my career. I am still deeply tied to K-12 education and K-12 science education. I am PI for an NSF CAREER project that - broadly - supports science teachers to take up and to teach about data science ideas. My wife is a librarian working at a public school and so I engage, well, deeply through her and that work.
:::

## Excel

![](/img/excel.jpg){width="70%"}

## Word Clouds

![](/img/word-cloud-fuglei.png){width="70%"}

## Text and science classroom data

Krist

![](/img/word-cloud-fuglei.png){width="70%"}

::: {.notes}

I came to data science through science education. I think of the spark being seeing students changing the colors of graphs they made in Excel, and seeing them relate to their product --- a small but meaningful relationship to data; claiming a relationship.

Another was seeing a colleague create an oft- (and I think correctly) bemoaned data visualization of text data, a word cloud. But they were so proud they created - coded - it. 

Still another was laying in bed in graduate school looking at R code I wrote, that worked. It was eye opening; I wasn't trained as a programmer, but I became, over time, interested, and then a bit obsessed, for years reading every book I could find (I think literally) on R, statistics using R.
:::

## Teacher education

UTK

## Public sentiment toward the NGSS

![](/img/tidylpa-website.png){width="70%"}

## Software

![](/img/tidylpa-website.png){width="70%"}

::: {.notes}

hard to explain to colleagues!
:::


## DSIEUR

![](/img/sentiment-model-eq.png){width="70%"}

::: {.notes}

easier to explain

not the best programmer, not the best teacher educator
:::

## CREDIBLE

![](/img/js-photo.jpg){width="70%"}

# Turn and talk

<div style="text-align:center">

***What was your path to education data science?***
</div>

# Timely Gaps and Problems

::: {.notes}

There are a lot! Let's highlight 3

:::

## Allied fields are doing good work

![](/img/eds-growth-abstract.png){width="70%"}


## Need for impact

NSF, tech backlash

## Data needs unmet

![](/img/intimidation-table.png){width="70%"}

QUOTE from workshop paper (Staudt Willet and Rosenberg, 2025)

# Cleveland's Challenge to Statisticians (and Us)

## What impacts the work of the data analyst?

Key quote about impact on practicing data analysts

## Data Science work

Table

## If education is the domain

What if we asked this for educational data science?

- Not only methods in an application area
- A consequential domain of practice
- A broader portfolio of work

Five possible avenues -- someone might work on some of these and not others. That is fine!

The claim is about what the field as a whole should count as central.

# Five Avenues (Among Others)

## There are examples

**These can bring us closer to educational practice, be distinct from "general" data science, and be an area of comparative advantage.**

1. Right-sized data problems
1. Data science education infrastructure
1. Research-practice partnerships around data education
1. Thinking about impact beyond journals articles
1. Teaching practitioners and researchers

## Right-sized data problems

Blair (Higher Education; Administrator): *A lot of my job is taking data that the school generates and helping the students to better understand their progress and also helping the faculty to better understand how to assess students’ progress and better support them... ***It’s not even a very sophisticated analysis, but it's aggregating it together and presenting it to them in a way that they can take action on it.**

Jamie (Industry, Learning Analytics): "And it's so so simple, **almost boringly simple.**"

Staudt Willet & Rosenberg (2024)

::: {.notes}
Starting with the problem 
Reich talks about this
:::

## Right-sized data problems

Agasisti and Bowers (2017) argued that the work of an educational data scientist would be “extremely delicate because it simultaneously necessitates the technical capacity for sophisticated data analysis and the sensibility for the specificities of the educational field” (p. 198).

> "In recent years, **advances in computing and information technologies have radically expanded the data available to researchers and professionals in a wide variety of domains**, from government and biotechnology, to business logistics and professional sports.

Baker and Yacef (2009, *JEDM*):

::: {.notes}
McFarland et al. (2021) argue that it is an umbrella --- broader than LAK and EDM
:::

## Data science education infrastructure

Teacher preparation - DS4E

Cleveland mentioned! 

Show survey findings 

<!-- NOTE TO SELF:
This can be part of “data science education infrastructure.”
Claim: educational data scientists are especially well positioned here because data education is our purview in the way laboratory investigations are partly biologists’ purview.
Could include tools, curricula, assessments, PD, and learning environments for data science education.

This may be the explicit Cleveland bridge.
Not: “Cleveland mentioned pedagogy, so we should mention teacher education.”
Rather: Cleveland shows how a field can ask what kinds of work its domain requires. Then ask that question for education.
-->

## Research-practice partnerships

RPP collaboratory

LEADS-DS

Trust, service --- and research

JS photo

<!-- NOTE TO SELF:
Possible fifth avenue. RPPs are not only a method or wrapper; they are a structure from which other work can come: right-sized problems, tools, studies, curricula, workshops, and public goods.
This can bridge to CREDIBLE later as an example of promise + difficulty.
-->

## Thinking about impact beyond journals articles

SEDA
EdTechBooks

## Teaching practitioners and researchers

Buffalo, Clemson, UTK

## Not only these five

1. Right-sized data problems
1. Data science education infrastructure
1. Research-practice partnerships around data education
1. Not ending with the journal article
1. Teaching practitioners and researchers
6. ... (from the presentations today and tomorrow)
7. ...

## A few words on AI (that haven't been said already)

AI is many things; this is _not_ a talk about AI

But to the key point, looking at these five, AI _can_ help us engage with practice more easily, or it can isolate us from avenues of work that can be impactful to teachers, learners, and educational systems

AI as an acceleration but also as a normal technology

# Back to the core question

***Is EDS a methodological program that takes education as a use case, or is it work that is situated in educational contexts and accountable to its educational impacts?***

## Turn and talk 

<div style="text-align:center">

If our northstar is "impacting the use of data in education", what should we prioritize?

</div>

# An Example

## Starting with practitioner needs

Jen photo

## Shiny + CODAP

![](/img/cld-full.png){width="70%"}

## It failed

![](/img/cld-class-bug.jpg){width="70%"}

## Then we fixed it 

![](/img/science-classroom.jpg){width="70%"}

## Local water quality data

E coli

## Fragile but impactful

This combines multiple possible directions - RPPs, tool building, right sized data, DS infrastructure, sharing public tols

This work is fragile, local, and contingent, but potentially impactful

I call this work practice-based education data science

# Conclusion

## A choice

***Is EDS a methodological program that takes education as a use case, or is it work that is situated in educational contexts and accountable to its educational impacts?***

My claim is practice-based education data science is a way to choose the former.

::: {.notes}
We might call practice-based education data science, simply, education data science

If education is the domain, practice is not optional; it is where the domain lives.

Educational data scientists are uniquely positioned to connect technical work, educational practice, practitioner capacity, and public value.
:::

## A call

Value work that emphasizes impact, partnership, and the data-related problems teachers, students, and others in education truly face.

Do work that shows us what this looks like!

## Acknowledgements 

Project CREDIBLE teachers around Knox County Schools, especially teacher leaders and Jen Sauer
Zhen Xu, Cody Pritchard, Leah Rosenbaum, and Bradley Holtz at the University of Tennessee Knoxville spo
NSF grant no. XX
